                             STANDARD OF REVIEW
      In the appeal concerning the interests of K.C.G., and the subsequent legal
matters involving M.P.G., Appellant vs. B.L.M. and C.T.A.M., Jasper County
Juvenile Office, Respondents, the standards of review that the Missouri Court of
Appeals, Southern District, should employ are delineated as follows:
1. DE NOVO REVIEW:
      For questions of jurisdiction and legal interpretation, which involve the
thorough examination of statutory provisions, case law, and constitutional
principles, a de novo standard of review is essential. When reviewing these
matters, the Appellate Court exercises an independent, non-deferential judgment.
This court must reassess the standard of review for legal issues presented without
presumption of correctness of the lower court’s ruling, ensuring that the legal
foundations dictating the outcome of the case are solidly in accordance with the
law and legal precedent.
2. SUBSTANTIAL EVIDENCE STANDARD:
      In reviewing the factual findings made by the trial Court, the standard of
review should shift to the substantial evidence test. This standard directs the
appellate court to affirm the lower court's determinations if the findings are
supported by substantial evidence on the record. Substantial evidence is such
relevant evidence that a reasonable mind might accept as adequate to support a
conclusion, even if it is possible to draw two inconsistent conclusions from the
evidence. (“Giha v. Garland, No. 15-73085 (9th Cir. 2021) :: Justia”) The
question is not whether the evidence could support a different conclusion but
whether it supports the conclusion that was reached by the trial Court.
3. CLEARLY ERRONEOUS STANDARD:
       When addressing the potential clear error in the trial court’s findings of fact,
it is warranted that such findings are left undisturbed unless the reviewing court, on
the entire evidence, is left with the definite and firm conviction that a mistake has
been made. This heightened degree of assurance in the trial court's factual accuracy
recognizes the trial judge’s role as the primary factfinder, having had the
opportunity to observe witness demeanor and to weigh the evidence presented
firsthand.
       The employment of these distinct standards of review acknowledges the dual
nature of appellate scrutiny, where legal issues are reviewed with precision and
independence, and factual determinations are accorded deference unless they fall
short of the evidentiary benchmark or are manifestly erroneous.

                                Argument Highlights
I. Novel Application of Ex Parte Young to Family Law In a groundbreaking
argument, I assert that the principles of the Ex Parte Young doctrine be extended to
encompass state family law proceedings. This unconventional approach seeks
judicial acknowledgment that constitutional violations within family law merit the
same rigorous scrutiny and injunctive relief historically granted in other federal
rights disputes.
II. Equal Parental Rights in Missouri law now recognizes paternal rights as being
equal to maternal rights. This appeal shows how the lower court's decision fails to
reflect these egalitarian principles and implores the Court to uphold the equitable
treatment of fathers within the jurisdiction, thus carving a path for similar cases.
III. Ensuring Due Process Challenges to due process in this case underlines the
importance of rigorous procedural safeguards, particularly where the risk of error
carries profound consequences on familial bonds. This Court's intervention is
imperative to affirm due process rights inherent in parental rights proceedings.
IV. The Intersection of Legal Representation and Self-Representation I illustrate
the perils faced when effective counsel is absent, and the right to
 self-representation is encumbered. This motion seeks the Court's injunction for
more explicit guidelines to be set, thus safeguarding these rights for all litigants,
especially those proceeding pro se.
V. Implications for Public Policy A favorable ruling will champion the
indispensable role of fathers in children's lives, reinforcing the state's commitment
to family integrity and the child's best interest — a ruling with profound
ramifications for family law jurisprudence.
 The precedent set forth in PEOPLE v. STULTZ by the Court of Appeals of
the State of New York in 2004 underscores the crucial role that due process
plays within judicial proceedings. In Stultz, the court rigorously examined the
procedural safeguards to ensure that the rights of the individuals were not
overshadowed by administrative expediency. In the present appeal, a similar
comprehensive examination is warranted to determine whether due process
was adequately provided during the proceedings culminating in the
termination of my parental rights.
Similarly, JENNINGS v. WOODFORD, a decision by the United States Court
of Appeals, Ninth Circuit in 2002, illustrates the pivotal nature of effective
legal representation in the context of protecting constitutional rights. The
Jennings court carefully parsed the impact of counsel's performance on the
outcome of the case. Here, the appellant contends that parallels can be drawn
regarding the inadequate representation received, thus casting significant
doubt on the fairness of the trial’s result.
In juxtaposition to our case, CLAUDIO v. SCULLY sheds light on the
principles of justice being equally meted out to all parties involved in a family
law context. In Claudio, the court meticulously addressed the balance between
state interests and individual rights, particularly in the context of parental
rights. The reflection of this balance is questioned in the case at bar, where the
scales of justice seem to have tilted unfavorably and prematurely against the
involvement and rights of a devoted father. Therefore, premised on the
aforementioned arguments and antecedenting case laws which highlight the
jurisprudence surrounding due process and effective counsel, the petitioner
respectfully submits this motion for the court’s reconsideration and requests
an en banc review to revisit and potentially rectify the decision pertaining to
the termination of parental rights.

termination order. This would prevent ongoing violations of the appellant's
 federal rights under 42 U.S.C. § 1983 and the protective doctrine established

B. Ineffective Assistance of Counsel The appellant respectfully asserts that the
assistance of counsel provided during the preliminary and subsequent hearings was
 not within the standard required by the Sixth Amendment as defined by the United
States Supreme Court in Strickland v. Washington. To establish a claim of
ineffective assistance of counsel, two requirements must be met: first, that
counsel’s performance was deficient; second, that the deficient performance
prejudiced the defense.
1. Deficient Performance
Counsel’s performance was deficient in several critical respects:
· Failure to file key motions: Counsel did not file motions essential to the
protection of the appellant's parental rights, including motions to challenge the
sufficiency of the evidence against the appellant and motions to suppress evidence
introduced in violation of the appellant's rights.
· Lack of preparation for hearings: On numerous occasions, counsel appeared at
hearings without having adequately prepared the case, failing to call important
witnesses, or present crucial evidence in defense of the appellant’s ability and right
to parent.
· Inadequate communication: Counsel failed to maintain reasonable
communication with the appellant, depriving him of the opportunity to make
informed decisions and to contribute effectively to his own defense.
2. Prejudice to the Defense
The deficient performance of counsel resulted in substantial prejudice to the
defense. Critical evidence favorable to the appellant was not introduced,
cross-examination of the state's witnesses was insufficient, and potential defenses
were not raised, thereby denying the appellant the opportunity for a fair trial.
According to McKaskle v. Wiggins,
this failure undermines the “adversary process that our system counts on to
produce just results.”
3. Applicable Authorities
In alignment with aforementioned standards, the appraisal of counsel’s
effectiveness must be grounded in an objective standard of reasonableness under
prevailing professional norms:
· In Luleff v. State and State ex rel. J.D.S. v. Edwards, the Missouri Supreme Court
emphasized the duty of defense counsel to engage in active advocacy and pursue a
strategic defense, obligations unmet in the present case.
 · The U.S. Supreme Court in Stanley v. Illinois underscored the importance of
process in parental rights adjudications - a process hampered by ineffective
counsel, leading to a failure in safeguarding the appellant’s due process rights as
mandated in Santosky v. Kramer.
C. Request for En Banc Review
The appellant respectfully requests an en banc review of this case to address
significant legal issues and discrepancies in the application of family law that
extend beyond the individual circumstance. The complexity and importance of the
issues raised herein warrant the collective expertise and judgment of the full bench
of the Missouri Court of Appeals.
1. Significance of Constitutional Issues
This appeal presents substantial constitutional questions that are pivotal for
determining parental rights not only within the purview of Missouri law but also in
interpreting the interface between state and federal jurisprudence regarding civil
rights under 42 U.S.C. § 1983 as elaborated in Green v. Mansour and due process
protections under the Fourteenth Amendment as expounded in Lassiter v.
Department of Social Services.
2. Broader Implications for Family Law
The issues at stake hold the potential to set vital precedents affecting the future
interpretation and application of parental rights in Missouri. They also raise broad
legal questions regarding the representation of pro se litigants and the effective
assistance of counsel — concerns which the Missouri Supreme Court has noted
require careful consideration as highlighted in Luleff v. State and J.A.R. v. D.G.R..
3. Discrepancies in Legal Interpretation
This case has uncovered meaningful inconsistencies in the judicial application of
established law which echo sentiments expressed in State ex rel. J. D. S. v.
Edwards. There is a clear need for authoritative clarification that can only be
provided by an en banc review. This will ensure uniformity and fairness in future
proceedings, a necessity underlined by the Missouri Court of Appeals cases such as
In re P.D. and In re Interest of V.C.N.C..
4. Need for En Banc Consideration
The appellant submits that the instant case presents questions of exceptional
importance. McKaskle v. Wiggins emphasizes the role of adversarial testing in
preserving the integrity of the process, which is particularly pertinent in cases
 regarding the termination of parental rights, an area where state and federal
interests often intersect as portrayed in Santosky v. Kramer and Stanley v. Illinois.
Given the presence of these broad and significant legal questions and their
implications for the public, as well as for the development of constitutional
principles within the context of state family law proceedings, an en banc hearing is
not only appropriate but essential. En banc consideration will allow for a more
detailed and comprehensive analysis of these issues and will help to develop a
coherent and fair body of law that adequately protects the rights of parents and the
welfare of children across the state.
Therefore, the appellant respectfully insists that an en banc review by this
Honorable Court is justified and requests the same in the interest of achieving the
most equitable resolution of this case.
D. Application of 42 U.S.C. § 1983
The appellant hereby asserts the application of 42 U.S.C. § 1983 regarding
violations of federal rights stemming from the unjust termination of parental rights.
The actions contested within the scope of this appeal infringe upon
Constitutionally protected rights and seek remedy through federal courts as per the
allowances of this statute.
1. Violation of Federal Rights
The termination of parental rights, as adjudicated, in this case, has transgressed
fundamental rights to due process and equal protection under the law. Such rights
are not merely procedural but are substantive in nature, affecting life's most
essential bond—between parent and child, as protected under the Fourteenth
Amendment and underpinned by the legal narrative developed in Santosky v.
Kramer.
2. State Actions Under 42 U.S.C. § 1983
When state actors, such as the juvenile office and its representatives, execute
actions that lead to termination of parental rights, these actions become subject to
scrutiny under 42 U.S.C. § 1983 if they contravene federal Constitutional rights.
The Appellant avers that in the course of these proceedings, actions rising to the
level of Constitutional violations have indeed occurred.
3. Relevance of Green v. Mansour, 474 U.S. 64, 106 S. Ct. 423 (1985) and
Pennsylvania v. Finley, 481 U.S. 551, 107 S. Ct. 1990 (1987)
The principles established in Green v. Mansour and Pennsylvania v. Finley
illuminate the considerations of this Court under 42 U.S.C. § 1983. While Green v.
 Mansour details the limits of declaratory relief where no ongoing violation of
federal law exists, the intensive involvement and interference by state actors in this
case mandate federal intervention for the continued breach. Similarly,
Pennsylvania v. Finley reinforces the idea that the right to appointed counsel
extends beyond the criminal courtroom, especially in proceedings that place a
heavy burden on an individual's rights — a stark similarity to the circumstances
faced by the appellant.
4. Missouri Precedents and Federal Rights
Missouri case law notably recognizes the gravity of terminating parental rights,
with the Supreme Court of Missouri in cases like In re S.M.H., No.
07-18-00148-CV (Tex. App. Nov. 6, 2019) requiring clear and convincing
evidence. When federal rights are compromised, as claimed here, an immediate
and federal judicial purview becomes necessary, invoking 42 U.S.C. § 1983 as a
defensive bulwark.
5. Conclusion
It is presented to this Honorable Court that the totality of actions culminating in the
termination of the appellant's parental rights not merely infringe upon state law but
elevate the dispute to a federal forum under the auspices of 42 U.S.C. § 1983. The
appellant invokes this statute to correct the injustices born out of state action,
rectify ongoing violations of federal law, and restore the appellant’s rights as a
parent that have been inappropriately severed.
In light of the above, the appellant respectfully requests this Court to grant an
injunction to halt the enforcement of the termination order under the weight of 42
U.S.C. § 1983 and to interpret the facts in light of the Constitutional guarantees
that frame the canvas of this nation's legal ethos.
In light of the above, the appellant respectfully requests this Court to grant an
injunction to halt the enforcement of the termination order under the weight of 42
U.S.C. § 1983 and to interpret the facts in light of the Constitutional guarantees
that frame the canvas of this nation's legal ethos.
F. Equitable Relief and Jurisdiction
In light of the new evidence and significant rehabilitation efforts of the appellant,
there is a compelling case for the issuance of equitable relief to prevent ongoing
violations of constitutional rights. Accordingly, the appellant invokes the court’s
jurisdiction to issue such relief, as is consistent with the principles established in
Ex parte Young and supported by a thread of precedential authorities which afford
 this honorable court the power to act justly in the face of potential miscarriages of
justice.
1. The Necessity of Injunctive Relief
The appellant has demonstrated a substantive change in circumstances that directly
affect the welfare of K.C.G. In accordance with the federal oversight afforded by
42 U.S.C. § 1983, and as illuminated through the lens of the Ex parte Young
doctrine, there is critical jurisdiction for a federal remedy against ongoing state
action perceived to violate the appellant's federal rights. The provision of
injunctive relief becomes not merely a provision of equity but a necessity to
prevent the ongoing deprivation of rights assured under the Fourteenth
Amendment.
2. Applicable Federal and State Authorities
The authority under Ex parte Young enables the court to issue injunctions when
constitutional rights are at stake, effectively halting state enforcement actions that
are against federal law. Furthermore, consistent with Missouri’s judicial
observations — as seen in State ex rel. State ex Rel. J. D. S. v. Edwards, 574
S.W.2d 405 (Mo. 1978) and In re P.D., No. 11-1698 (W. Va. Jun. 25, 2012) —
this call for suspension of the state's decision is rooted in the need to preserve the
appellant's rights pending a closer examination of the adverse effects that such
statutory actions may impose on him.
3. Supported by the Guidelines in Ex parte Young
Ex parte Young provides a clear basis for the federal court to enjoin state officials
from enforcing state laws that are in violation of the federal Constitution. In this
context, the termination of the appellant’s parental rights without due regard for his
recent improvements and compelling evidence not only challenges the doctrines
established in cases like Lassiter v. Department of Social Services and Santosky v.
Kramer but also suggests a departure from the rigors of procedural due process.

3. Issuance of an injunction to halt the enforcement of the termination order

4. Any other relief that this Court deems just and proper

of parental rights, a decision appealable as of right. Furthermore, the issuance of
injunctive relief against ongoing violations of Federal Rights, including those
rights protected under 42 U.S.C. § 1983, invokes this Court's Jurisdiction under the
EX PARTE YOUNG DOCTRINE, which allows Federal Courts to enjoin state
officials from violating Federal Law, as established by the United States Supreme
Court in Ex parte Young, 209 U.S. 123 (1908). This motion timely follows the
entry of judgment, adhering to the timeframe established by Rule 84.04 of the
Missouri Rules of Appellate Procedure, ensuring that the Court may exercise its
jurisdiction to provide relief as necessary.
                             STANDARD OF REVIEW
      In the appeal concerning the interests of K.C.G., and the subsequent legal
matters involving M.P.G., Appellant vs. B.L.M. and C.T.A.M., Jasper County
Juvenile Office, Respondents, the standards of review that the Missouri Court of
Appeals, Southern District, should employ are delineated as follows:
1. DE NOVO REVIEW:
      For questions of jurisdiction and legal interpretation, which involve the
thorough examination of statutory provisions, case law, and constitutional
principles, a de novo standard of review is essential. When reviewing these
matters, the Appellate Court exercises an independent, non-deferential judgment.
This court must reassess the standard of review for legal issues presented without
presumption of correctness of the lower court’s ruling, ensuring that the legal
foundations dictating the outcome of the case are solidly in accordance with the
law and legal precedent.
2. SUBSTANTIAL EVIDENCE STANDARD:
      In reviewing the factual findings made by the trial Court, the standard of
review should shift to the substantial evidence test. This standard directs the
appellate court to affirm the lower court's determinations if the findings are
supported by substantial evidence on the record. Substantial evidence is such
relevant evidence that a reasonable mind might accept as adequate to support a
conclusion, even if it is possible to draw two inconsistent conclusions from the
evidence. (“Giha v. Garland, No. 15-73085 (9th Cir. 2021) :: Justia”) The
question is not whether the evidence could support a different conclusion but
whether it supports the conclusion that was reached by the trial Court.
3. CLEARLY ERRONEOUS STANDARD:
       When addressing the potential clear error in the trial court’s findings of fact,
it is warranted that such findings are left undisturbed unless the reviewing court, on
the entire evidence, is left with the definite and firm conviction that a mistake has
been made. This heightened degree of assurance in the trial court's factual accuracy
recognizes the trial judge’s role as the primary factfinder, having had the
opportunity to observe witness demeanor and to weigh the evidence presented
firsthand.
       The employment of these distinct standards of review acknowledges the dual
nature of appellate scrutiny, where legal issues are reviewed with precision and
independence, and factual determinations are accorded deference unless they fall
short of the evidentiary benchmark or are manifestly erroneous.

                ACCUSATION OF CHEMICAL DEPENDENCY
Additionally, essential to this motion is the invalid accusation of chemical
dependency leveled against me without substantial evidence or the benefit of
robust legal defense, which questions the veracity and sufficiency of the evidence
presented at trial .
   ● KOHL v. LEHLBACK 160 U.S. 293 (1895)
16 S. Ct. 304
New Evidence and Procedural Irregularities Subsequent to the Court's decision,
new evidence has surfaced that warrants reevaluation. Concurrently, procedural
missteps, including inadequate legal counsel and misdirected communications,
have prejudiced the judicial outcome.
   ● Allegations concerning my history with substances have been predicated on
     fragile testimonies and reports, devoid of the crucial context which
     elucidates a journey of resilience and reform, culminating in my current
     abstinent lifestyle
   ● Procedural missteps marked by a premature motion to reconsider induce a
     pressing need for a retrospective evaluation, while the allegations of
     chemical dependency, void of substantiated proof, further augment of my
     petition for this Court to grant rehearing.
                         JURISDICTION AND VENUE:
The federal courts are implored, under 42 U.S.C. § 1983 and as per the doctrine of
Ex-Partee Young, to provide relief from ongoing violations of due process
concerning the enforcement of the State of Missouri's termination of my parental
rights. In addition, the jurisdiction of the abuse and neglect proceedings is
questioned, drawing upon:
• STANLEY v. ILLINOIS, (405 U.S. 645, U.S. Supreme Court, 1972), which
upholds the rights of parents in judicial proceedings, directly impacting the context
of the present case involving the rights and assertions made by an unwed father.
• Jurisdictional Concerns and Miscommunications
The administrative grievance of jurisdictional overstep looms over this case. My
perpetual presence in my son's life rebut this overreach, and the inadvertent
 misplacement of crucial communications hamstrung my capability to navigate
through my legal predicaments.
• I respectfully invoke the Court to employ its mandated jurisdiction under federal
law to afford an equitable resolution to the rights infringed upon within the state
judiciary's confines.
G. Denial of parental rights:
In re Stephanie M., F04CP15003270B (Conn. Super. Ct. Nov. 30, 2017) and In
re In re Marilyn H, 5 Cal.4th 295, 19 Cal. Rptr. 2d 544, 851 P.2d 826 (Cal.
1993) were My rights were restricted solely based on the mother's actions, and
challenge this decision, arguing that it is an inappropriate punitive measure against
me when I was not the parent responsible for the neglect.
E. Implications of the ICPC Assessment Not Performed
Youngberg v. Romeo, 457 U.S. 307, 102 S. Ct. 2452 (1982) An ICPC
assessment's glaring omission has potentially exposed my son to unwarranted
perils of well documented sexual and physical abuse in his current adoptive
placement within the foster system, a scenario that could have been mitigated or
preempted with appropriate diligence. (Exhibit emails to and from case supervisor
)
                                Argument Highlights
I. Novel Application of Ex Parte Young to Family Law In a groundbreaking
argument, I assert that the principles of the Ex Parte Young doctrine be extended to
encompass state family law proceedings. This unconventional approach seeks
judicial acknowledgment that constitutional violations within family law merit the
same rigorous scrutiny and injunctive relief historically granted in other federal
rights disputes.
II. Equal Parental Rights in Missouri law now recognizes paternal rights as being
equal to maternal rights. This appeal shows how the lower court's decision fails to
reflect these egalitarian principles and implores the Court to uphold the equitable
treatment of fathers within the jurisdiction, thus carving a path for similar cases.
III. Ensuring Due Process Challenges to due process in this case underlines the
importance of rigorous procedural safeguards, particularly where the risk of error
carries profound consequences on familial bonds. This Court's intervention is
imperative to affirm due process rights inherent in parental rights proceedings.
IV. The Intersection of Legal Representation and Self-Representation I illustrate
the perils faced when effective counsel is absent, and the right to
 self-representation is encumbered. This motion seeks the Court's injunction for
more explicit guidelines to be set, thus safeguarding these rights for all litigants,
especially those proceeding pro se.
V. Implications for Public Policy A favorable ruling will champion the
indispensable role of fathers in children's lives, reinforcing the state's commitment
to family integrity and the child's best interest — a ruling with profound
ramifications for family law jurisprudence.
 The precedent set forth in PEOPLE v. STULTZ by the Court of Appeals of
the State of New York in 2004 underscores the crucial role that due process
plays within judicial proceedings. In Stultz, the court rigorously examined the
procedural safeguards to ensure that the rights of the individuals were not
overshadowed by administrative expediency. In the present appeal, a similar
comprehensive examination is warranted to determine whether due process
was adequately provided during the proceedings culminating in the
termination of my parental rights.
Similarly, JENNINGS v. WOODFORD, a decision by the United States Court
of Appeals, Ninth Circuit in 2002, illustrates the pivotal nature of effective
legal representation in the context of protecting constitutional rights. The
Jennings court carefully parsed the impact of counsel's performance on the
outcome of the case. Here, the appellant contends that parallels can be drawn
regarding the inadequate representation received, thus casting significant
doubt on the fairness of the trial’s result.
In juxtaposition to our case, CLAUDIO v. SCULLY sheds light on the
principles of justice being equally meted out to all parties involved in a family
law context. In Claudio, the court meticulously addressed the balance between
state interests and individual rights, particularly in the context of parental
rights. The reflection of this balance is questioned in the case at bar, where the
scales of justice seem to have tilted unfavorably and prematurely against the
involvement and rights of a devoted father. Therefore, premised on the
aforementioned arguments and antecedenting case laws which highlight the
jurisprudence surrounding due process and effective counsel, the petitioner
respectfully submits this motion for the court’s reconsideration and requests
an en banc review to revisit and potentially rectify the decision pertaining to
the termination of parental rights.
Coupled with the presented new evidence, the aforementioned cases assert the
appellant’s stance that significant procedural irregularities occurred. Such
occurrences testify to the need for an injunction to halt the enforcement of the
termination order. This would prevent ongoing violations of the appellant's
 federal rights under 42 U.S.C. § 1983 and the protective doctrine established
in EX PARTE Young.
The petitioner submits that granting these requests is not only in the best
interest of K.C.G. but also upholds the integrity of legal proceedings and the
fundamental principles of due process and parental rights—cornerstones of
our judicial system and society as a wholle



 CLAIM FOR RELIEF:
The failure to conduct an ICPC assessment or consider placement with the
undersigned Petitioner, despite pre-established shared custody, constitutes a
continuous violation of due process rights as guaranteed by the Fourteenth.
Amendment. Furthermore, Merging the aforementioned variables fabricates an
undeniable tapestry of judicial oversight and systemic faltering. Cumulatively,
these factors have precipitated an injustice that beckons redress. This appeal is
constructed not solely on a father's fervent plea but is firmly rooted in the
principles of justice and equity that embody the judicial mantle this Court
espouses, A clarion call is made to this Honorable Court to issue a declaratory
judgment affirming this case's role as a potential vanguard for constitutional
applications in family law. Further, I beseech the Court for an injunction preventing
the enforcement of my parental rights termination, pending a full and fair
consideration of the issues I've presented. Finally, the Court is invited to grant any
additional relief it deems appropriate, heralding a new epoch in the judicial
valorization of fatherhood rights. [Fair notice] Granting this motion and
considering the legal positions articulated would not only serve the ends of legal
rectification for one father but would also potentially illumine the path for future
jurisprudence, safeguarding the integrity of family and the sanctity of parental
rights.


A. Due Process and Parental Rights Appellate Argues the importance of
maintaining procedural due process and rely on STANLEY v. ILLINOIS, (405
U.S. 645, U.S. Supreme Court, 1972), Santosky v. Kramer: Referenced within the
Bess v. Barroso citation, these cases set a high standard of proof (clear and
convincing evidence) for the termination of parental rights,;
B. Ineffective Assistance of Counsel The appellant respectfully asserts that the
assistance of counsel provided during the preliminary and subsequent hearings was
 not within the standard required by the Sixth Amendment as defined by the United
States Supreme Court in Strickland v. Washington. To establish a claim of
ineffective assistance of counsel, two requirements must be met: first, that
counsel’s performance was deficient; second, that the deficient performance
prejudiced the defense.
1. Deficient Performance
Counsel’s performance was deficient in several critical respects:
· Failure to file key motions: Counsel did not file motions essential to the
protection of the appellant's parental rights, including motions to challenge the
sufficiency of the evidence against the appellant and motions to suppress evidence
introduced in violation of the appellant's rights.
· Lack of preparation for hearings: On numerous occasions, counsel appeared at
hearings without having adequately prepared the case, failing to call important
witnesses, or present crucial evidence in defense of the appellant’s ability and right
to parent.
· Inadequate communication: Counsel failed to maintain reasonable
communication with the appellant, depriving him of the opportunity to make
informed decisions and to contribute effectively to his own defense.
2. Prejudice to the Defense
The deficient performance of counsel resulted in substantial prejudice to the
defense. Critical evidence favorable to the appellant was not introduced,
cross-examination of the state's witnesses was insufficient, and potential defenses
were not raised, thereby denying the appellant the opportunity for a fair trial.
According to McKaskle v. Wiggins,
this failure undermines the “adversary process that our system counts on to
produce just results.”
3. Applicable Authorities
In alignment with aforementioned standards, the appraisal of counsel’s
effectiveness must be grounded in an objective standard of reasonableness under
prevailing professional norms:
· In Luleff v. State and State ex rel. J.D.S. v. Edwards, the Missouri Supreme Court
emphasized the duty of defense counsel to engage in active advocacy and pursue a
strategic defense, obligations unmet in the present case.
 · The U.S. Supreme Court in Stanley v. Illinois underscored the importance of
process in parental rights adjudications - a process hampered by ineffective
counsel, leading to a failure in safeguarding the appellant’s due process rights as
mandated in Santosky v. Kramer.
C. Request for En Banc Review
The appellant respectfully requests an en banc review of this case to address
significant legal issues and discrepancies in the application of family law that
extend beyond the individual circumstance. The complexity and importance of the
issues raised herein warrant the collective expertise and judgment of the full bench
of the Missouri Court of Appeals.
1. Significance of Constitutional Issues
This appeal presents substantial constitutional questions that are pivotal for
determining parental rights not only within the purview of Missouri law but also in
interpreting the interface between state and federal jurisprudence regarding civil
rights under 42 U.S.C. § 1983 as elaborated in Green v. Mansour and due process
protections under the Fourteenth Amendment as expounded in Lassiter v.
Department of Social Services.
2. Broader Implications for Family Law
The issues at stake hold the potential to set vital precedents affecting the future
interpretation and application of parental rights in Missouri. They also raise broad
legal questions regarding the representation of pro se litigants and the effective
assistance of counsel — concerns which the Missouri Supreme Court has noted
require careful consideration as highlighted in Luleff v. State and J.A.R. v. D.G.R..
3. Discrepancies in Legal Interpretation
This case has uncovered meaningful inconsistencies in the judicial application of
established law which echo sentiments expressed in State ex rel. J. D. S. v.
Edwards. There is a clear need for authoritative clarification that can only be
provided by an en banc review. This will ensure uniformity and fairness in future
proceedings, a necessity underlined by the Missouri Court of Appeals cases such as
In re P.D. and In re Interest of V.C.N.C..
4. Need for En Banc Consideration
The appellant submits that the instant case presents questions of exceptional
importance. McKaskle v. Wiggins emphasizes the role of adversarial testing in
preserving the integrity of the process, which is particularly pertinent in cases
 regarding the termination of parental rights, an area where state and federal
interests often intersect as portrayed in Santosky v. Kramer and Stanley v. Illinois.
Given the presence of these broad and significant legal questions and their
implications for the public, as well as for the development of constitutional
principles within the context of state family law proceedings, an en banc hearing is
not only appropriate but essential. En banc consideration will allow for a more
detailed and comprehensive analysis of these issues and will help to develop a
coherent and fair body of law that adequately protects the rights of parents and the
welfare of children across the state.
Therefore, the appellant respectfully insists that an en banc review by this
Honorable Court is justified and requests the same in the interest of achieving the
most equitable resolution of this case.
D. Application of 42 U.S.C. § 1983
The appellant hereby asserts the application of 42 U.S.C. § 1983 regarding
violations of federal rights stemming from the unjust termination of parental rights.
The actions contested within the scope of this appeal infringe upon
Constitutionally protected rights and seek remedy through federal courts as per the
allowances of this statute.
1. Violation of Federal Rights
The termination of parental rights, as adjudicated, in this case, has transgressed
fundamental rights to due process and equal protection under the law. Such rights
are not merely procedural but are substantive in nature, affecting life's most
essential bond—between parent and child, as protected under the Fourteenth
Amendment and underpinned by the legal narrative developed in Santosky v.
Kramer.
2. State Actions Under 42 U.S.C. § 1983
When state actors, such as the juvenile office and its representatives, execute
actions that lead to termination of parental rights, these actions become subject to
scrutiny under 42 U.S.C. § 1983 if they contravene federal Constitutional rights.
The Appellant avers that in the course of these proceedings, actions rising to the
level of Constitutional violations have indeed occurred.
3. Relevance of Green v. Mansour, 474 U.S. 64, 106 S. Ct. 423 (1985) and
Pennsylvania v. Finley, 481 U.S. 551, 107 S. Ct. 1990 (1987)
The principles established in Green v. Mansour and Pennsylvania v. Finley
illuminate the considerations of this Court under 42 U.S.C. § 1983. While Green v.
 Mansour details the limits of declaratory relief where no ongoing violation of
federal law exists, the intensive involvement and interference by state actors in this
case mandate federal intervention for the continued breach. Similarly,
Pennsylvania v. Finley reinforces the idea that the right to appointed counsel
extends beyond the criminal courtroom, especially in proceedings that place a
heavy burden on an individual's rights — a stark similarity to the circumstances
faced by the appellant.
4. Missouri Precedents and Federal Rights
Missouri case law notably recognizes the gravity of terminating parental rights,
with the Supreme Court of Missouri in cases like In re S.M.H., No.
07-18-00148-CV (Tex. App. Nov. 6, 2019) requiring clear and convincing
evidence. When federal rights are compromised, as claimed here, an immediate
and federal judicial purview becomes necessary, invoking 42 U.S.C. § 1983 as a
defensive bulwark.
5. Conclusion
It is presented to this Honorable Court that the totality of actions culminating in the
termination of the appellant's parental rights not merely infringe upon state law but
elevate the dispute to a federal forum under the auspices of 42 U.S.C. § 1983. The
appellant invokes this statute to correct the injustices born out of state action,
rectify ongoing violations of federal law, and restore the appellant’s rights as a
parent that have been inappropriately severed.
In light of the above, the appellant respectfully requests this Court to grant an
injunction to halt the enforcement of the termination order under the weight of 42
U.S.C. § 1983 and to interpret the facts in light of the Constitutional guarantees
that frame the canvas of this nation's legal ethos.
In light of the above, the appellant respectfully requests this Court to grant an
injunction to halt the enforcement of the termination order under the weight of 42
U.S.C. § 1983 and to interpret the facts in light of the Constitutional guarantees
that frame the canvas of this nation's legal ethos.
F. Equitable Relief and Jurisdiction
In light of the new evidence and significant rehabilitation efforts of the appellant,
there is a compelling case for the issuance of equitable relief to prevent ongoing
violations of constitutional rights. Accordingly, the appellant invokes the court’s
jurisdiction to issue such relief, as is consistent with the principles established in
Ex parte Young and supported by a thread of precedential authorities which afford
 this honorable court the power to act justly in the face of potential miscarriages of
justice.
1. The Necessity of Injunctive Relief
The appellant has demonstrated a substantive change in circumstances that directly
affect the welfare of K.C.G. In accordance with the federal oversight afforded by
42 U.S.C. § 1983, and as illuminated through the lens of the Ex parte Young
doctrine, there is critical jurisdiction for a federal remedy against ongoing state
action perceived to violate the appellant's federal rights. The provision of
injunctive relief becomes not merely a provision of equity but a necessity to
prevent the ongoing deprivation of rights assured under the Fourteenth
Amendment.
2. Applicable Federal and State Authorities
The authority under Ex parte Young enables the court to issue injunctions when
constitutional rights are at stake, effectively halting state enforcement actions that
are against federal law. Furthermore, consistent with Missouri’s judicial
observations — as seen in State ex rel. State ex Rel. J. D. S. v. Edwards, 574
S.W.2d 405 (Mo. 1978) and In re P.D., No. 11-1698 (W. Va. Jun. 25, 2012) —
this call for suspension of the state's decision is rooted in the need to preserve the
appellant's rights pending a closer examination of the adverse effects that such
statutory actions may impose on him.
3. Supported by the Guidelines in Ex parte Young
Ex parte Young provides a clear basis for the federal court to enjoin state officials
from enforcing state laws that are in violation of the federal Constitution. In this
context, the termination of the appellant’s parental rights without due regard for his
recent improvements and compelling evidence not only challenges the doctrines
established in cases like Lassiter v. Department of Social Services and Santosky v.
Kramer but also suggests a departure from the rigors of procedural due process.
---
VI. CONCLUSION AND PRAYER FOR RELIEF
In conclusion, the appellant respectfully requests a reevaluation of the decision by
this Honorable Court based on the principles of fairness, the presentation of new
evidence, and relevant federal and state law considerations.
Prayers for relief include:
 1. Reassessment of the decision terminating parental rights based on updated
evidence and due process considerations.
2. The convening of an en banc court to review significant legal questions
presented in this case.
3. Issuance of an injunction to halt the enforcement of the termination order
pending a reevaluation of this case under the proper application of the law.
4. Any other relief that this Court deems just and proper
Based on the above-mentioned authorities, alongside compelling facts and
circumstances apparent in the record, Petitioner respectfully requests that this
Honorable Court grant the Motion to Reconsider. Petitioner seeks the opportunity
to address the deficiencies noted herein and achieve an outcome that embodies the
just, equitable principles that underpin our legal system. For the reasons stated, I
respectfully request that the court grants this motion for reconsideration. I appeal to
the Court's equitable conscience to deliberate on the augments herein with the
urgency and gravity they warrant. It is not merely the father's right that rests in the
balance but the unfolding future of Kody Goodman, over whose welfare I remain
steadfastly vigilant. Ergo, I beseech the Court to sanction this motion, to initiate an
en banc review with alacrity, and should it be deemed essential, to endorse a
transfer to a judicial forum equipped to extend this case the breadth of
consideration it merits. We put our faith in the hope that the Court's wisdom will
navigate us to a just and compassionate resolution. Failing to do so not only affect
my parental rights but more importantly, impacts the well-being and welfare of my
son, Kody Goodman. Embracing the intricacies of this case, the Court has the
esteemed duty of delivering justice not only to an individual but potentially to the
very structure of family law jurisprudence. The outcomes sought herein stand to
thoroughly embody the noblest legal principles, offering sturdy protection for
paternal rights and setting a precedent of considerable legal worth.
. Respectfully submitted,
Matthew Goodman

FW_-ALTON-GOODMAN-CORRECTED-NOTICE-OF-MT:
Subject: RE: FW: ALTON GOODMAN CORRECTED NOTICE OF MTM HRG RESET 21-2/3
From: "Emmons, Jennifer" <Jennifer.Emmons@dss.mo.gov>
To: 'Mark Goodman' <mpgooderman@gmail.com>
Date: Tue, 05 Apr 2022 13:35:50 +0000

We do not have trials in juvenile court. I just provide the court with an update and the Judge will decide if the case goal should be changed or not. This isn’t a criminal case so there is no jury or anything like that, you aren’t being convicted of any crimes. Our jurisdiction is over the children. I can provide you with another application for a court appointed attorney if you would like, but am not sure if the court would grant it in time as the hearing is set for Thursday and the Judge made it clear that we are moving forward with the hearing one way or another.

Jennifer Emmons
Social Services Unit Supervisor
(417)629-3896

I have provided you with a list of what the court expects more than once and it has been discussed with you during meetings. At this point, you have been given 15 months to work on those things in order to reunify. Federal statute dictates our time frames and states that if a child is in custody for 15 out of the most recent 22 months and we do not have compelling reasons to continue working reunification, we are required to seek a different permanency option for the children. You can let the Judge know your position at the hearing on Thursday as well. Kody is not attending school currently. He will begin Pre-K in the fall in Marionville. I’m not sure if they have located a new pediatrician for him but I can ask and let you know what I find out. You are also welcome to ask placement when you see them at visits.

Jennifer Emmons

Missouri Court of Appeals
                                       Southern District

IN THE INTEREST OF K.C.G.                       )

           Respondents,                         )                  MAY 02 2024
                                                )
                                                                CRAIG A. STREET, CLERK
vs.                                             )             MISSOURI COURT OF APPEALS
                                                )                 SOUTHERN DISTRICT
M.P.G.,                                         )
                                                )
           Appellant.                           )


                                            ORDER

     Now on this day, the court takes up appellant’s “MOTION FOR RECONSIDERATION,
REQUEST FOR EN BANC REVIEW TRANSFER, AND PETITION FOR INJUNCTIVE RELIEF FOR
ONGOING VIOLATION OF FEDERAL RIGHTS UNDER 42 U.S.C. § 1983 AND THE EX PARTE
YOUNG DOCTRINE,” filed April 20, 2024. The court construes said filing as a motion for
rehearing and application for transfer. See Missouri Supreme Court Rules 83.02 and 84.17(a)(1).
Having fully considered the same, the court hereby denies the filing in its entirety. See Missouri
Supreme Court Rule 83.04. The court further takes up and denies any and all other requests for
relief by appellant that have not previously been ruled upon by the court.

Subject: RE: Visitation,work,attorney

Hi Matt,

I’m adding Shania to this e-mail as well since she’s the case manager.

Regarding the attorney, if you are still working with a private attorney, you are still welcome to complete an application for the court to appoint you an attorney to represent you in the adoption proceedings and termination of parental rights. I have included an application to this e-mail. You would just need to complete it and return it to us or directly to the Juvenile Office.

As far as Zoom visits and such, Shania did talk to me about trying to arrange this with placement. I don’t think anyone on the team would have an issue with that. Just be mindful that you likely won’t be able to keep both boys’ attention for a full hour, so those visits may not last the full time depending on their attention span. Shania and I talked about ways we could work with that as well to keep the boys in a setting where they can sit and talk for more than a couple minutes so we are trying to be mindful of that as well.

As far as your response to the social study, I would process that with your attorney-whether it’s the same one or different one. You are welcome to contact the Clerk’s office to inquire about this as well. I’m not sure what their practice is when parents try to get things docketed on their own when they are represented by counsel.

You may send any kind of letters, gifts, etc. to our office and put it to Shania’s attention so she can get it to Kody. Please do not send cash or gift cards however, as in the past this has created issues in some cases.

Thank You,

Jennifer Emmons
Social Services Unit Supervisor
(417)629-3896
